planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; also easily started indoors 6 weeks before last frost. Self-sows abundantly once established — remove seedlings where not wanted.

directions: Direct sow in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; inland sea oats is one of the most valuable native ornamental grasses specifically because it thrives in deep shade — a condition that defeats virtually all other ornamental grasses. The flat, bamboo-like leaf blades form a graceful arching clump, but the showstopper is the hanging, oat-like seed clusters that emerge in summer, dangling from slender wiry branches, and persist into winter — turning from green to copper-tan to warm bronze, rattling pleasantly in the wind. It grows naturally along stream banks and in bottomland forests, giving it outstanding tolerance of both shade and periodic wet soils. Self-sows prolifically once established and can become weedy; deadhead if you want to limit spread. The seeds are relished by songbirds in fall.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in late summer to early fall in moist to wet, acidic soil at the water's edge or in shallow water; fall planting allows roots to establish before winter dormancy and spring bloom.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; this native species is the parent of the beloved Louisiana iris hybrid group, growing naturally in swamps, bayou margins, ditches, and wet flatwoods of the Gulf Coast from Florida to Texas. The large, elegant blue-violet flowers (occasionally varying to pink or white) in spring are extraordinary among native wildflowers for their sophisticated form and vivid color. Louisiana iris hybrids have expanded the color range to include burgundy, yellow, white, pink, and bicolors — if you want color variety, seek out named Louisiana iris hybrids from specialty nurseries; for native restoration, stick with the true species. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested. Plant at pond margins, in rain gardens, or in any consistently wet spot.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in spring after last frost, or in fall in zone 8–10 where they are hardy; the bulbs naturalize quickly and bloom most reliably after summer rain events.

directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; pink rain lily is the most commonly grown zephyr lily in the South, producing soft rose-pink funnel-shaped flowers that emerge just days after summer and fall rainfall — an event that earns them the name 'rain lily.' The bulbs go dormant through winter and dry summers, then spring to life reliably after soaking rains from June through October. In zone 9–10 they may also bloom sporadically through winter with sufficient moisture. Plant in drifts of 20 or more for the most impactful display along path edges, in rock gardens, or at the front of borders. The bulbs naturalize readily and multiply over years into showy colonies. In zones colder than 7, lift and store bulbs dry through winter.

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, alkaline to slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; a single tree is self-fertile, unlike most fruit trees.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to light shade; Mexican plum is the finest native flowering tree of the Texas limestone country, erupting in a cloud of fragrant white flowers in late February to March before any leaves emerge — often the first tree in bloom at the end of winter. A single tree is self-fertile and produces 1–2 inch dark purple plums in late summer with sweet-tart, deeply flavorful flesh excellent for jams, jellies, wine, and fresh eating. Unlike the suckering, thicket-forming Chickasaw plum, Mexican plum grows as a single-trunked tree and does not sucker; it is well-adapted to alkaline limestone and caliche soils where most fruit trees struggle. Mockingbirds and other thrushes consume the ripe fruit. Drought tolerant once established.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade; one of the few native evergreen shrubs that performs in partial to full shade in the Southeast.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; wild olive is the native North American member of the same genus as the prized Asian fragrant sweet olive (O. fragrans), and though its white flowers in late spring are smaller and less flamboyant, they carry a delicate, sweet fragrance and are borne abundantly on large shrubs or small trees in woodland settings. It grows naturally in moist, shaded coastal plain forests and ravine forests of the Southeast, making it well-suited to garden use under tall trees where it provides refined, glossy-leaved evergreen structure year-round. The small, dark blue berry-like drupes ripen in fall and provide wildlife food. Slow growing but extremely long-lived; excellent as a foundation shrub or informal hedge in shade.
